topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed into the pale expanse of my desk. I stared at the 'D-'. It wasn't just a mark; it was a physical weight settling in my chest, heavy and cold. My hands, usually so steady when gripping a pen, trembled slightly as I traced the poorly scrawled critique. A hollow ache bloomed just behind my ribs, a dull throb that made focusing on anything else feel like trying to lift a boulder. I pushed the textbook away, the sound of the paper sliding across the wood unnervingly loud in the quiet room. I just wanted to curl up under a thick blanket and disappear until the feeling subsided.

[story 2]

"Thirty-two percent," the professor had said, his voice flat, devoid of any sympathy. I felt the air evacuate my lungs in a sudden, shallow rush. My gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon light was turning a bruised shade of grey. Every muscle in my neck felt tight, as if I were constantly bracing for an impact that never quite arrived. I picked up my worn notebook, but the pages looked alien, the words refusing to cohere into anything meaningful. I ran a fingertip over the binding, a restless energy churning beneath my skin, a profound sense of emptiness settling deep within my core.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback three times, the words swimming before my eyes like disturbed water. A slow, heavy sigh escaped me, a sound too large for the small space I occupied. My shoulders slumped forward, the tension draining out of me until I felt boneless. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a profound weariness, as if I had run a marathon I hadn't even known I was training for. The room seemed muted, the colors muted, mirroring the dull, heavy feeling that clung to my limbs. I didn't move to fix anything; I simply sat, letting the quiet emptiness fill the space where excitement or even frustration used to reside.
